g.data-narration: Welcome to Timestamp Ordering - a powerful concurrency control technique used in modern database systems.
g.text: university - BCS403 Database Management System | Module 5

g.data-narration: Timestamp Ordering uses unique time values to determine transaction execution order, avoiding traditional locking mechanisms.
g.text: An optimistic concurrency control technique that determines transaction order
using unique timestamps instead of locks. Older timestamps have higher priority.

g.data-narration: Unlike lock-based methods that prevent conflicts, timestamp ordering allows transactions to proceed freely, checking only for violations.
g.text: Traditional locking is pessimistic - it prevents conflicts. TSO is optimistic -
it allows execution and only checks for violations. Best when conflicts are rare.

g.data-narration: A timestamp is a unique identifier - typically a counter or system time - assigned when a transaction begins execution.
g.text: A timestamp is a unique identifier assigned to each transaction at the start.
Older timestamps (smaller values) have HIGHER priority - they arrived first.

g.data-narration: For each data item Q, the system maintains Read_Timestamp and Write_Timestamp - the largest timestamps of transactions that successfully accessed it.
g.text: Two Timestamp Values per Data Item
Largest timestamp of any transaction that successfully read Q
Largest timestamp of any transaction that successfully wrote Q
Example: If Write_Timestamp(X) = 25, transaction T₂₅ was the last to write item X.

g.data-narration: When reading: if your timestamp is older than the last write, you must rollback. Otherwise, read succeeds and update timestamp.
g.text: Read succeeds! Update: Read_Timestamp(Q) = max(Read_Timestamp(Q), TS(Tᵢ))
Transaction Tᵢ is ROLLED BACK - reading overwritten value!
Example: T₁₀ reads X. If Write_Timestamp(X)=20, then 10<20 → T₁ is rolled back!

g.data-narration: For writes: check both read and write timestamps. If younger transactions accessed the data, you must rollback.
g.text: ✓ SUCCESS: TS(Tᵢ) ≥ Read_Timestamp(Q) AND TS(Tᵢ) ≥ Write_Timestamp(Q)
Younger transaction already read or wrote Q

g.data-narration: Thomas' Write Rule optimizes the protocol - instead of rejecting certain writes, we ignore them as no-ops, improving throughput.
g.text: Instead of rolling back, certain writes can be treated as no-ops (ignored):

g.data-narration: Strict TO uses strict inequalities to prevent cascading rollbacks - transactions only access committed data.
g.text: Prevents cascading rollbacks by using strict greater-than (>) conditions:

g.data-narration: Let's trace through an example: T1 reads X, T2 writes X, T1 writes X. Watch what happens with timestamps.
g.text: Schedule: R1(X), W2(X,200), W1(X,150) | TS(T1)=10, TS(T2)=20 | Initial X=100
T1 tries to write after T2 already overwrote X! T1 is aborted.

g.data-narration: In this example, Thomas' Write Rule allows T1 to continue instead of rolling back - the write is simply ignored.
g.text: 📝 Example 2: Thomas' Write Rule in Action
T1 attempts Write(Y,100) on item Y
Thomas' Write Rule: T1 continues (no rollback)!
Since TS(T1) < Write_Timestamp(Y), we ignore the write as a no-op, avoiding unnecessary rollback.

g.data-narration: This schedule shows how timestamp ordering validates operations step by step, detecting conflicts that violate serializability.
g.text: Schedule: R1(X), R2(X), W1(X), R3(Y), W2(Y), W3(Y)

g.data-narration: Remember these essential formulas: read succeeds if timestamp ≥ write timestamp, write needs both conditions satisfied.
g.text: WRITE: TS ≥ Read_TS AND TS ≥ Write_TS

g.data-narration: Key exam points: timestamp ordering avoids deadlocks but can cause cascading rollbacks; older timestamps have priority.
